title: Probabilistic size-and-shape functional mixed models

reviews.summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.
reviews.strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.
reviews.weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.
reviews.questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.
reviews.limitations: The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.
reviews.strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.
reviews.questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?
reviews.limitations: See above

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7

reviews.summary: The paper considers uncertainty quantification for one-dimensional regression tasks, where the observations are noisy. A rather advanced additive model considering invariances under space-time unitary transformations is proposted. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of the approach over other state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.strengths:
- Due to the strong modeling assumptions, on synthethic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as GPs) may fail. 
- The methods seems to be useful to analyze the Berkley growth spurt data set.
reviews.weaknesses:
- It is unclear whether the work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience and the considered topic and datasets feel more of a niche. 
- Despite the well-written introduction, the paper was quite difficult to understand due to the advanced math and notations -- after reading it I am still quite unsure why I should really care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference.
reviews.questions: I am slightly puzzled as of why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper - the work may not get the appreciation here it deserves. I spent quite some time on trying to understand the paper, but its motivations and applications to wider machine learning remain unclear. Perhaps the paper may be better suited towards a statistics journal, such as the ones referenced often in the paper?   If more general motivations are clarified, I am considering to change my score, but accessibility issues are remaining.
reviews.limitations: All limitations are adequately addressed.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper considers uncertainty quantification for one-dimensional regression tasks, where the observations are noisy. A rather advanced additive model considering invariances under space-time unitary transformations is proposted. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of the approach over other state-of-the-art methods.

Strengths:
- Due to the strong modeling assumptions, on synthethic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as GPs) may fail. 
- The methods seems to be useful to analyze the Berkley growth spurt data set.

Weaknesses:
- It is unclear whether the work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience and the considered topic and datasets feel more of a niche. 
- Despite the well-written introduction, the paper was quite difficult to understand due to the advanced math and notations -- after reading it I am still quite unsure why I should really care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference.

Questions:
I am slightly puzzled as of why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper - the work may not get the appreciation here it deserves. I spent quite some time on trying to understand the paper, but its motivations and applications to wider machine learning remain unclear. Perhaps the paper may be better suited towards a statistics journal, such as the ones referenced often in the paper?   If more general motivations are clarified, I am considering to change my score, but accessibility issues are remaining.

Limitations:
All limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5

Strengths:
- Due to the strong modeling assumptions, on synthethic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as GPs) may fail. 
- The methods seems to be useful to analyze the Berkley growth spurt data set.

Weaknesses:

- Despite the well-written introduction, the paper was quite difficult to understand due to the advanced math and notations -- after reading it I am still quite unsure why I should really care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference.

Questions:
I am slightly puzzled as of why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper - the work may not get the appreciation here it deserves. I spent quite some time on trying to understand the paper, but its motivations and applications to wider machine learning remain unclear. Perhaps the paper may be better suited towards a statistics journal, such as the ones referenced often in the paper?   If more general motivations are clarified, I am considering to change my score, but accessibility issues are remaining.

Limitations:
All limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5

reviews.summary:
For the problem of the reliable recovery of a fixed effect function $\mu$, this paper focuses on sampling from and summarizing the posterior distribution of a fixed effect function $\mu$ in a functional mixed model with random object-level phase and amplitude components, without a finite-rank covariance assumption on the error process.

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.
reviews.strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
1)	It seems that we should give different prior models for different functions.
2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.
reviews.questions:
1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
For the problem of the reliable recovery of a fixed effect function $\mu$, this paper focuses on sampling from and summarizing the posterior distribution of a fixed effect function $\mu$ in a functional mixed model with random object-level phase and amplitude components, without a finite-rank covariance assumption on the error process.

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.

Strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
1)	It seems that we should give different prior models for different functions.
2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.

Questions:
1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
Expanded Review:

Summary:  
This paper addresses the challenging problem of reliably recovering a fixed‐effect function μ in a functional mixed‐effects framework where both amplitude (vi) and phase (γi) variation are present, and measurement error is unrestricted (i.e., not assumed to have a finite‐rank covariance). Unlike standard value‐preserving time‐warping approaches, the authors introduce a norm‐preserving (“size‐and‐shape”) action of the diffeomorphism group Γ on the Hilbert space L², which induces an infinite‐dimensional unitary transformation Dγ. Under their Bayesian model, γi becomes a size‐and‐shape‐preserving random effect (capturing warping and local scaling), while vi and additive noise account for shape‐altering deviations. Informative priors on γi (two options: a one‐parameter family PM1 and a flexible Dirichlet‐process‐based discretization PM2) regularize the inference, and posterior sampling is carried out via an MCMC algorithm with Metropolis–Hastings updates whose acceptance ratios are derived in detail (Appendix D). Extensive simulations—both under the authors’ own generative model (with Bf = Br = 6 basis functions, σc² = 0.25, σ² = 0.1, n = 30) and under the frequentist warpMix model—and real data applications (Berkeley growth rates, PQRST ECG complexes, plus five additional datasets in Appendix I) demonstrate that the proposed Bayesian size‐and‐shape mixed model outperforms warpMix and other state‐of‐the‐art approaches in terms of mean‐function recovery error and provides well‐calibrated uncertainty quantification.

Post‐rebuttal Note: The authors’ rebuttal clarified the flexibility of the two prior models, elaborated on the MCMC implementation (including proposal adaptation and convergence diagnostics in Appendix F), and provided additional comparisons (FPCA basis, sensitivity analyses, runtime profiling). These responses address most of my initial concerns.

Strengths:  
1) Novel Geometric Modeling. By leveraging the norm‐preserving action of Γ, the authors shift inference from the classical shape (value‐preserving) to size‐and‐shape, an invariant property under space–time diffeomorphisms (Section 2). This perspective is both mathematically elegant and practically effective, as confirmed by simulations in Figure 2 and real‐data examples in Figure 4.  
2) Rigorous Derivations. The paper provides a thorough derivation of the marginal likelihood (Equation 6; Appendix C) and of the Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio for all parameters, including the nonparametric prior on γi (Appendix D). This level of detail is rare in functional mixed models with nonparametric phase, and it will be valuable to practitioners.  
3) Empirical Validation and Diagnostics. The authors carry out extensive numerical experiments (Section 4) across simulated and real datasets. They compare four Bayesian variants (Models 1‐F, 1‐B, 2‐F, 2‐B) to warpMix and a Bayesian registration‐only method (BRFC), reporting both visual uncertainty (posterior samples, credible bands) and quantitative error metrics (Δµ). Convergence diagnostics (Appendix F) support MCMC reliability, and sensitivity analyses (Appendix H) show robustness to over‐ and under‐specification of basis dimension (Bf, Br) and prior concentration θγ.

Weaknesses:  
1) Choice of Prior Models for Different Data Modalities. The paper presents two generic prior specifications for γi (PM1 and PM2) and applies them across a diverse set of functional datasets (growth curves, ECGs, gait, etc.). While these cover a spectrum from simple to flexible, it remains unclear whether more specialized priors (e.g., spline‐based priors with higher continuity, multi‐scale GP priors) would yield further gains in particular applications. A more systematic guideline for choosing between PM1 and PM2—or even designing bespoke priors tailored to certain domains—would strengthen the practical guidance of the paper.  
2) Parameter Sensitivity of θγ and Computational Cost. Although Appendix H investigates the effect of under‐/over‐specifying θγ for PM2 on a simulated example, a broader sweep of θγ (beyond {1, 30, 100}) on real data could illuminate how strongly the posterior of γi and μ depends on this concentration parameter. Moreover, Section 5 briefly notes that running 300 K MCMC iterations in MATLAB took ~1.5 hours for n=30, T=50 under PM1/2. A more detailed breakdown—per‐iteration cost, scaling with n, T, Bf, Br, and comparisons to warpMix runtime—would help potential users assess feasibility on larger datasets.

Questions for the Authors:  
1) Neural‐Network‐Based Alternatives. Modern deep generative models (e.g., Bayesian neural operators, deep Gaussian processes) can flexibly recover functions under warping. How does the proposed Bayesian size‐and‐shape model compare—both in accuracy and uncertainty quantification—to neural architectures trained to jointly register and reconstruct curves? Are there regimes (e.g., very large n, high‐dimensional function spaces) where neural methods might be preferable, and how might one integrate their feature extraction with the unitary‐group priors introduced here?  
2) Recovering Arbitrary Functions under a Fixed Model (Eq. 3). Equation (3) posits a fixed orthonormal basis (ϕk, ˜ϕk) together with random warping by γi. In practice, μ may have local features not well captured by the chosen basis (e.g., sharp edges, discontinuities). Could you clarify how the model’s expressivity scales with Bf, Br and whether extensions (e.g., adaptive basis learning, multi‐resolution wavelet bases) could allow recovery of more arbitrary μ? Is there a principled way to let Bf and Br be random and inferred within the Bayesian framework?

Rating: 5 (Accept)

reviews.summary: In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.
reviews.strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.
reviews.weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.
reviews.questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?
reviews.limitations: The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Rating:
5

title: Chain of Preference Optimization: Improving Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in LLMs

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO) to improve reasoning in LLMs. CPO utilizes preference data generated during ToT to fine-tune models. This approach improves reasoning without increasing inference complexity.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of CPO is interesting.
2. CPO achieves enhanced performance without increasing inference complexity.
3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. While CPO is effective across several tasks, its performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark, remains unexplored.
2. Fine-tuning with reasoning paths generated by ToT may reduce the diversity of generated paths. A systematic investigation into how the generated reasoning paths change before and after fine-tuning is needed.
3. The effectiveness of CPO is heavily dependent on the quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. If ToT produces suboptimal paths, the preference data might not be as beneficial.
4. The paper does not adequately address the applicability of CPO to more advanced models, such as LLaMA3.
5. While CPO enhances performance, the paper lacks a deep analysis of the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. Understanding why certain paths are preferred could provide additional insights and improve trust in the model’s decisions.
reviews.limitations: Yes

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
Expanded Review:

Summary:  
This paper proposes Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO), a novel fine-tuning framework that leverages the intermediate preference signals naturally generated by the Tree-of-Thought (ToT) search process to improve Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in large language models (LLMs). Concretely, the authors collect per-step “preferred” and “dispreferred” thought pairs from the ToT search tree—marking all alternative reasoning continuations that were pruned during breadth‐first search (BFS) as dispreferred—and then apply the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) objective to align the model’s CoT generation with these preferences. Extensive experiments on seven datasets spanning question answering (Bamboogle, WikiMultiHopQA, HotpotQA), fact verification (FEVER, Feverous, VitaminC), and arithmetic reasoning (SVAMP) show that CPO yields an average accuracy gain of 4.3 % over standard CoT prompting, outperforms the strong tree‐search‐supervised fine‐tuning baseline (TS-SFT) by 2.7 % on average, and matches or exceeds full ToT performance while retaining the low inference latency of CoT (∼57× faster than ToT as reported in Table 1). The paper is clearly written, the method is well motivated by the limitations of single-path CoT and high-latency ToT, and the empirical analysis—including component ablations in Figure 3 and ablation curves across different models and datasets in Appendix B—paints a compelling overall picture of CPO’s strengths.

Strengths:  
1.  The idea of leveraging per-step preference information from the ToT search, rather than relying solely on the single best path, is both novel and conceptually elegant.  The authors correctly observe that every pruning decision in ToT implicitly encodes a preference over alternative thoughts, and they exploit this underutilized signal in a systematic way.  This insight moves beyond prior SFT approaches [11,12] that discard non-optimal branches entirely.  
2.  CPO delivers consistent performance improvements without incurring extra inference cost.  As shown in Table 1, fine‐tuned CPO models achieve comparable or even better accuracy than full ToT while maintaining the sub-second per‐instance latencies associated with CoT greedy decoding.  This efficiency makes CPO practical for real‐time or large‐scale deployments where ToT’s latency (often on the order of thousands of seconds per instance) is prohibitive.  
3.  The paper is well organized and generally easy to follow.  The diagrams in Figures 1 and 2 effectively illustrate the differences between CoT, ToT, and CPO, and the authors provide clear pseudocode descriptions of thought generation, evaluation, and preference pair collection.  Key hyperparameters (e.g., number of thoughts k=10, top-n pruning size n=5, DPO regularization β=0.1) are documented, and the implementation details in Section 5.1 and Appendix A facilitate reproducibility.

Weaknesses:  
1.  While CPO is shown to be effective on a range of QA, fact‐verification, and arithmetic reasoning tasks, the paper does not explore its behavior on more challenging benchmarks such as MATH or GSM-8K, which feature deeper multi‐step reasoning and more varied question formats.  Given that CPO’s preference signals are derived from the ToT tree, it would be helpful to evaluate whether these per‐step preferences still guide the model effectively on highly compositional or symbolic reasoning problems.  
2.  The fine‐tuning process in CPO is driven by reasoning paths discovered via ToT, which by design focus on the highest‐scoring branches.  This raises the concern that diversity in the model’s CoT outputs may shrink after training, potentially limiting novel or creative reasoning alternatives.  A more systematic analysis of how output distributions change—measuring, for example, n-gram diversity or thought‐variation entropy before and after CPO—would strengthen the paper’s claims about generalization and robustness.  
3.  CPO’s success hinges on the quality of the ToT‐generated reasoning paths.  If the ToT search itself is misled by heuristic evaluation scores (e.g., the “likely/impossible” classification described in Section 4.1) or if ToT fails to explore certain valid branches, then the derived preference data may encode suboptimal or even incorrect reasoning patterns.  An empirical investigation of how noisy or adversarial pruning affects CPO’s fine‐tuning—perhaps by injecting flawed evaluation prompts or constraining tree‐search breadth—would illuminate the method’s sensitivity to the underlying search quality.  
4.  The applicability of CPO to larger or more recent LLMs—such as Llama 3, GPT‐4, or other advanced self‐supervised models—is not addressed.  It remains unclear whether the same DPO‐based approach scales to models with tens or hundreds of billions of parameters, or whether certain adaptations (e.g., larger LoRA ranks or different β settings) would be necessary.  A brief discussion or small‐scale pilot on a larger model would enhance the paper’s practical relevance.  
5.  Although CPO optimizes the model to prefer higher‐quality reasoning steps, the paper does not include a deep interpretability analysis of why certain paths are preferred.  For example, examining token‐level gradients, conducting path‐similarity clustering, or presenting case studies that highlight how preference alignment corrects specific reasoning errors would provide valuable insights into the model’s decision dynamics and bolster user trust in the optimized reasoning chains.

Overall Recommendation (Rating: 6):  
This paper makes a meaningful contribution by introducing a new way to harness the latent preference data generated in tree‐of‐thought search to improve chain‐of‐thought reasoning without sacrificing inference efficiency.  Despite some open questions about model diversity, robustness to search noise, and scaling to larger LLMs, the methodological innovation is solid, the empirical gains are nontrivial across multiple tasks, and the manuscript is accessible and reproducible.  I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary: The paper presents a novel method to enhance the performance of LLMs in complex problem-solving tasks using Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO). The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The CPO method aligns each step of the CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT, leveraging the inherent preference information in the tree-search process. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.
reviews.strengths: The paper presents a novel method, Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), that significantly enhances the CoT reasoning ability of LLMs without increasing the inference load. It offers a convincing comparison with the ToT method, demonstrating that CPO substantially reduces inference time, as evidenced by the decrease in latency from 1749s/ins to 38s/ins on Llama2-7B. Furthermore, the paper shows that CPO not only matches but surpasses the ToT baseline in terms of accuracy across multiple datasets. This indicates that CPO effectively fine-tunes LLMs to generate more optimal reasoning paths, thereby improving their performance in complex problem-solving tasks. The research is significant as it provides a practical solution to the challenge of balancing inference complexity and reasoning quality in LLMs.
reviews.weaknesses: The paper presents a promising approach to fine-tuning LLMs using CPO. However, there is a significant gap in the evaluation of the potential impacts of this fine-tuning process on other abilities of the pretrained LLMs. This omission could leave readers questioning the broader implications and versatility of the proposed method. Future work should consider a comprehensive evaluation of the fine-tuning process to fully understand its effects on the LLMs' overall performance.
reviews.questions:
LN35, what is the advantage of CPO comared to existing MCTS methods? And why?

LN117, have you considered online RL that regenerates the chain-of-preferences using the updated LLM?

Table1, I'm wondering how does the method perform on common reasoning tasks like GSM8K and ProofWriter?
reviews.limitations: Yes.

The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.

Strengths:

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
Expanded Review

Summary:  
This paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO), a novel fine-tuning framework that leverages the search tree generated by Tree-of-Thought (ToT) decoding to improve Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in large language models (LLMs). Rather than relying solely on the single best reasoning path, CPO constructs paired preference data at each reasoning step by marking the thoughts on the ToT-selected path as preferred and the remaining sibling thoughts as dispreferred. Training proceeds with direct preference optimization (DPO), aligning the LLM’s step-by-step generation probabilities with the preferences discovered by ToT. The authors evaluate CPO on seven benchmarks spanning three reasoning categories—question answering (Bamboogle, WikiMultiHopQA, HotpotQA), fact verification (FEVER, FEVEROUS, VitaminC), and arithmetic reasoning (SVAMP)—and with three base models (LLaMA2-7B/13B, Mistral-7B). Experimental results show that CPO yields an average accuracy gain of 4.3% over the standard CoT approach, matches or exceeds the performance of ToT (while being 57.5× faster at inference), and outperforms a supervised fine-tuning baseline (TS-SFT) by 2.7% on average. The paper also provides thorough component-wise analyses, including ablations on the selection of dispreferred thoughts, the number of training instances, data mixture sensitivity, and comparisons to full-path preference optimization (FPO) to highlight the importance of per-step supervision.

Strengths:  
1. Novelty and Practical Impact: CPO presents a creative way to import the deliberation strength of ToT into a training-only procedure. By shifting the computational burden to the fine-tuning phase, CPO maintains CoT’s low-latency inference while capturing multi-path preference signals. For example, on LLaMA2-7B, CPO’s inference latency is 38 s per instance compared to 1749 s for ToT, yet it outperforms ToT in overall accuracy on several tasks (e.g., 32.0% vs. 29.6% on Bamboogle).  
2. Comprehensive Evaluation: The authors test CPO across a wide variety of reasoning tasks and base models, demonstrating consistent gains—up to +9.7% absolute improvement on individual benchmarks. They report both accuracy and latency, and include statistical significance testing (p < 0.01) for key comparisons. The paper also details dataset splits, hyperparameters (e.g., β=0.1, k=10, n=5), training regimen (4 epochs, LoRA rank 8, AdamW optimizer), and hardware used (A100 GPUs), ensuring reproducibility.  
3. Rich Ablation Studies: The paper investigates how the choice of dispreferred thoughts (selecting the lowest-scoring versus all non-preferred thoughts) has minimal effect, validates the necessity of per-step preference signals over full-path DPO (FPO suffers from longest common prefix gradient cancellation), and explores data requirements—showing that around 120 training instances suffice to surpass the base model. These analyses provide clear guidance for practitioners.  
4. Clear Theoretical Motivation: The discussion of the LCP gradient cancellation issue in full-path preference optimization is insightful. By formalizing how gradients on shared prefixes cancel out (Equation 16–17) and demonstrating empirically that FPO underperforms, the authors convincingly argue for CPO’s chain-level supervision.  

Weaknesses:  
While the paper convincingly shows that CPO improves reasoning performance on targeted tasks, it leaves unexamined how this fine-tuning affects other capabilities of the pretrained LLM. For instance, does CPO tuning degrade open-ended generation quality, factual consistency in non-reasoning domains, or the model’s performance on downstream tasks like summarization or dialogue? The absence of such evaluations creates uncertainty about unintended side-effects or trade-offs introduced by preference alignment. A more comprehensive evaluation—perhaps measuring zero-shot performance on common NLP benchmarks or assessing perplexity on held-out corpora—would help clarify the broader implications and versatility of CPO.

Questions for the Authors:  
1. LN35: Can you elaborate on the advantages of CPO compared to existing Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS) methods used at inference time? Specifically, how does directly optimizing with preference pairs differ from training separate policy and value networks in AlphaZero-style approaches?  
2. LN117: Have you considered an online reinforcement learning protocol that regenerates the chain-of-preference data using the updated LLM after each fine-tuning iteration? How might such a procedure impact convergence speed and final accuracy?  
3. Table 1: Beyond the seven datasets you evaluated, how does CPO perform on other widely used reasoning tasks such as GSM8K (grade-school math) or ProofWriter (deductive reasoning)? Including these would help benchmark CPO against prior CoT improvements in the literature.  
4. Training Overhead: Generating ToT-based preference data is computationally expensive. Could you quantify the total training time and GPU hours required to produce the ToT trees for all 300 instances per dataset? This would help readers assess the practical cost/benefit trade-off.  
5. Generalization: In your data mixture experiments (Table 2), CPO trained on mixed reasoning types yielded similar gains as single-task training. Have you tried pretraining CPO on one domain (e.g., arithmetic) and then fine-tuning on another (e.g., fact verification) to test cross-domain transfer of preference signals?

Limitations:  
The authors acknowledge that CPO relies on ToT to generate training signals, which is time-consuming—even if only used offline. The current work is limited to text-only LLMs and a small set of downstream reasoning tasks. Extending CPO to multimodal models or to tasks such as machine translation, text classification, and dialogue remains future work. Moreover, the potential for misuse—such as aligning LLMs to undesirable preference criteria—highlights the need for ethical safeguards and transparency in any real-world deployment.

Rating: 6  
Overall, this paper makes a solid and novel contribution to the efficient distillation of tree-based reasoning into a lightweight inference framework. The experimental results and analyses are convincing for the targeted reasoning tasks. However, a more thorough assessment of the broader impacts of fine-tuning on the LLM’s general capabilities would strengthen the work and address questions about versatility and unintended side-effects.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper presents a novel method to enhance the performance of LLMs in complex problem-solving tasks using Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO). The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The CPO method aligns each step of the CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT, leveraging the inherent preference information in the tree-search process. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.

Strengths:
The paper presents a novel method, Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), that significantly enhances the CoT reasoning ability of LLMs without increasing the inference load. It offers a convincing comparison with the ToT method, demonstrating that CPO substantially reduces inference time, as evidenced by the decrease in latency from 1749s/ins to 38s/ins on Llama2-7B. Furthermore, the paper shows that CPO not only matches but surpasses the ToT baseline in terms of accuracy across multiple datasets. This indicates that CPO effectively fine-tunes LLMs to generate more optimal reasoning paths, thereby improving their performance in complex problem-solving tasks. The research is significant as it provides a practical solution to the challenge of balancing inference complexity and reasoning quality in LLMs.

Weaknesses:
The paper presents a promising approach to fine-tuning LLMs using CPO. However, there is a significant gap in the evaluation of the potential impacts of this fine-tuning process on other abilities of the pretrained LLMs. This omission could leave readers questioning the broader implications and versatility of the proposed method. Future work should consider a comprehensive evaluation of the fine-tuning process to fully understand its effects on the LLMs' overall performance.

Questions:
LN35, what is the advantage of CPO comared to existing MCTS methods? And why?

LN117, have you considered online RL that regenerates the chain-of-preferences using the updated LLM?

Table1, I'm wondering how does the method perform on common reasoning tasks like GSM8K and ProofWriter?

Limitations:
Yes.

Rating:
6

reviews.summary:
This paper presents Chain-of-preference optimization (CPO) a self-supervised learning extension of Tree of Thought (ToT). Rather than use ToT during test time, which takes exponentially longer than end-to-end sampling, this paper proposes to use ToT at training time to annotate data for DPO fine-tuning and then use the DPO-tuned model end-to-end at test time. Notably, the DPO annotations are performed by the untuned model itself being prompted to label inferences as either useful or not. This differs from other approaches that are trained on only full successful reasoning paths.

For certain QA and reasoning tasks, the paper presents experimental evidence that CPO yields stronger models than either (1) fine-tuning the same LM on positive examples (TS-SFT) as done in previous papers or (2) running regular chain-of-thought prompting.
reviews.strengths:
1. The approach is an intuitive and straightforward improvement to multi-hop reasoning that that is much faster than tree-based inference procedures.
2. The approach is appealing as it doesn't rely on correct labels nor external LLMs other than the LLM being fine-tuned on its own preference data.
3. The methodology is well explained and easily reproducible.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. While the authors run evaluation over several datasets, they only consider 300 questions per dataset which is quite small. A stronger evaluation would use the whole test sets.
2. Details about the baseline implementation are unclear. The authors refer to both "TS-SFT" and "TS-LLM" (l.206) which in the original paper refer to different things-- SFT refers to the model fine-tuned on training examples with reasoning traces pulled from both a gold-annotated dataset and/or reasoning traces from the model that led to correct answers. However, TS-LLM in the original paper refers to the result of an iterative refinement process.
3. The choice of datasets is somewhat odd. E.g. HotpotQA is meant to be evaluated against support documents, which might explain the very low scores. They also compare against approaches (ToT, TS-SFT) that were not evaluated on any of the QA or Fact Verification tasks under consideration.  This paper did _not_ consider the Game of 24 or GSM datasets, which both ToT and TS-SFT did evaluate. 

Missing references:
1. Khalifa et al 2023: [GRACE: Discriminator-Guided Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://aclanthology.org/2023.findings-emnlp.1022.pdf)
2. Li et al 2023: [Learning Math Reasoning from Self-Sampled Correct and Partially-Correct Solutions](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=4D4TSJE6-K)
reviews.questions:
* What metrics did you use for the QA datasets? many of them use more than exact match, e.g. HotpotQA uses F1.
* The discussion around ablations in 5.3 is unclear. Are the trends consistent across different models and datasets? 
* It is odd to have only considered up to 200 instances for constructing preference pairs. Why is this experiment limited to such a small maximum? The difference between e.g. 160 and 200 seems much less important than the difference between 200 and 1000 or 1000 and 5000. 
* How many total preference pairs do you end up training on? It would be helpful to include this number in the main body of the paper.
* How stable is this approach to different prompts for state evaluation? Did you experiment with other prompts?
reviews.limitations: yes

Weaknesses:

2. Details about the baseline implementation are unclear. The authors refer to both "TS-SFT" and "TS-LLM" (l.206) which in the original paper refer to different things-- SFT refers to the model fine-tuned on training examples with reasoning traces pulled from both a gold-annotated dataset and/or reasoning traces from the model that led to correct answers. However, TS-LLM in the original paper refers to the result of an iterative refinement process.
3. The choice of datasets is somewhat odd. E.g. HotpotQA is meant to be evaluated against support documents, which might explain the very low scores. They also compare against approaches (ToT, TS-SFT) that were not evaluated on any of the QA or Fact Verification tasks under consideration.  This paper did _not_ consider the Game of 24 or GSM datasets, which both ToT and TS-SFT did evaluate. 

Missing references:
1. Khalifa et al 2023: [GRACE: Discriminator-Guided Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://aclanthology.org/2023.findings-emnlp.1022.pdf)
2. Li et al 2023: [Learning Math Reasoning from Self-Sampled Correct and Partially-Correct Solutions](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=4D4TSJE6-K)

Questions:

yes

6

reviews.summary: This paper presents a method called Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO) that fine-tunes large language models (LLMs) using the tree-of-thought (ToT) method to improve the performance of chain-of-thought (CoT) decoding. CPO aligns each step of CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT by leveraging preference information in the tree-search process, resulting in similar or better performance than CoT alone while avoiding the significant inference burden. Experimental results demonstrate its effectiveness.
reviews.strengths:
Innovation: The method of constructing preference dataset with ToT has a certain novelty.

Writing quality:  Your paper is well-written, with clear and precise language and a smooth flow of ideas.  The structure is reasonable,  and the logic is sound, making it very enjoyable to read.

Experimental analysis: This paper has made a full experimental demonstration.
reviews.weaknesses:
1.  The experiment lacked a stronger baseline comparison.  In my opinion, the author's method belongs to the self-imporvement methods, so it should be compared with such typical methods, such as the ReST and self-rewarding mentioned by the author in related work.

2.  The ToT approach to dataset construction appears to be computationally expensive.

3.  the author does not seem to provide a task-specific prompt templates (although the general guideline prompt is mentioned in line 141).  This is not necessary, but I think the quality of building a preference dataset can be seriously affected by the prompt template of evaluation.
reviews.questions:
1. Why use DPO instead of other preference optimization algorithms?
2. Explain why the experiment lacks different self-imporvement baseline algorithms.
3. There may be a large number of negative samples in the preference dataset constructed by ToT making the dataset unbalanced.  May I ask whether this will have a negative impact on the training stage?
reviews.limitations: The limitations of the study and the possible negative social impact have been well documented by the authors.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a method called Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO) that fine-tunes large language models (LLMs) using the tree-of-thought (ToT) method to improve the performance of chain-of-thought (CoT) decoding. CPO aligns each step of CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT by leveraging preference information in the tree-search process, resulting in similar or better performance than CoT alone while avoiding the significant inference burden. Experimental results demonstrate its effectiveness.

Strengths:
Innovation: The method of constructing preference dataset with ToT has a certain novelty.

Writing quality:  Your paper is well-written, with clear and precise language and a smooth flow of ideas.  The structure is reasonable,  and the logic is sound, making it very enjoyable to read.

Experimental analysis: This paper has made a full experimental demonstration.

Weaknesses:
1.  The experiment lacked a stronger baseline comparison.  In my opinion, the author's method belongs to the self-imporvement methods, so it should be compared with such typical methods, such as the ReST and self-rewarding mentioned by the author in related work.

2.  The ToT approach to dataset construction appears to be computationally expensive.

3.  the author does not seem to provide a task-specific prompt templates (although the general guideline prompt is mentioned in line 141).  This is not necessary, but I think the quality of building a preference dataset can be seriously affected by the prompt template of evaluation.

Questions:
1. Why use DPO instead of other preference optimization algorithms?
2. Explain why the experiment lacks different self-imporvement baseline algorithms.
3. There may be a large number of negative samples in the preference dataset constructed by ToT making the dataset unbalanced.  May I ask whether this will have a negative impact on the training stage?

Limitations:
The limitations of the study and the possible negative social impact have been well documented by the authors.

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper introduces Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), a novel fine-tuning framework that leverages the tree-of-thought (ToT) search process to generate paired preference signals at each reasoning step. By applying Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to align chain-of-thought (CoT) generations with the higher-quality reasoning paths discovered via ToT, CPO improves multi-step reasoning performance without incurring the inference-time overhead associated with ToT. Extensive experiments on seven reasoning datasets—Bamboogle, WikiMultiHopQA, HotpotQA, Fever, Feverous, Vitaminc, and SVAMP—demonstrate that CPO yields an average accuracy gain of 4.3% over base CoT models, outperforms the TS-SFT baseline by 2.7% on average, and matches or exceeds ToT’s performance while maintaining ∼57× lower inference latency.

Strengths:  
1. Innovation: The idea of harvesting fine-grained, per-step preference signals from the ToT search tree to supervise CoT fine-tuning is original. Rather than only using the single best ToT path, CPO exploits all intermediate “winning” and “losing” thoughts, which enriches the supervision signal and addresses the gradient cancellation issue inherent in full-path preference methods.  
2. Writing Quality: The manuscript is very well organized, with clear sections, numbered equations, and a logical progression from motivation to method to experiments. Figures 1 and 2 effectively illustrate the differences among CoT, ToT, and CPO, and the description of DPO in Section 3.3 is concise yet complete.  
3. Experimental Coverage: The authors evaluate CPO across a diverse set of tasks (question answering, fact verification, arithmetic reasoning) and multiple backbone models (LLaMA2-7B/13B, Mistral-7B). They also include latency measurements to substantiate the claim that inference complexity remains low. The component-wise analyses in Figure 3 (e.g., dispreferred selection strategies, data scale, per-step supervision) provide valuable insights into why CPO works.  

Weaknesses:  
1. Missing Strong Baselines from Self-Improvement Literature:  
   The experimental section compares CPO against CoT, ToT, and TS-SFT, but omits other prominent self-improvement methods such as ReST (Reinforced Self-Training) [33, 34] and LLM self-rewarding [28]. Although these methods rely on either external reward models or seed labeled data, the authors themselves reference them in Section 2. A direct comparison on at least one dataset would help clarify CPO’s advantages over these approaches, especially since ReST also generates synthetic feedback signals and iteratively fine-tunes the model.  
2. Computational Expense of ToT-Based Data Generation:  
   While CPO shifts the search cost to the training phase, constructing the preference dataset still requires running ToT’s tree search on up to 300 instances per dataset, which is nontrivial. The authors acknowledge this limitation in Section 7 but do not quantify the total compute overhead of dataset generation. Including the wall-clock time or GPU-hour estimate for the ToT data collection process would help readers assess the practicality of adopting CPO in resource-constrained settings.  
3. Lack of Task-Specific Prompt Templates for Evaluation:  
   The paper mentions a “general guideline prompt” for state evaluation (line 141) and states that task-specific demonstrations follow the format of CoT, but the precise prompt templates are not provided. Since prompt wording can significantly affect the quality of LLM evaluations, the absence of these templates (e.g., in Appendix A) may hinder reproducibility. Providing concrete examples of the evaluation prompt, including how demonstrations are selected and formatted for each dataset, would strengthen the methodological clarity.  

Questions for the Authors:  
1. Why was DPO chosen over other RLHF or preference-learning algorithms (e.g., PPO-based RLHF, DAgger-like imitation learning, or the recently proposed token-level DPO variants [48])? Could you comment on scenarios where DPO may be preferable or where alternative methods might offer advantages?  
2. Could you elaborate on why ReST and self-rewarding baselines were omitted from the main experiments? Do you expect CPO to outperform them, and if so, have you conducted any preliminary comparisons?  
3. The ToT process generates many non-optimal (dispreferred) thoughts per step, potentially resulting in a highly imbalanced preference dataset. Have you observed any negative effects from this imbalance during training (e.g., stability issues or bias toward negative samples)? If so, how did you mitigate them?  

Additional Observations and Suggestions:  
- In Section 5.2, Table 1 shows that CPO achieves similar or better accuracy than ToT while reducing inference latency by ∼57× on average. It would be useful to break down these latency gains by dataset or by reasoning-step length to illustrate where the efficiency benefits are most pronounced.  
- The ablation on dispreferred selection strategies (Figure 3(a)) finds minimal performance differences among “Lowest,” “Lower,” and “All” strategies. This suggests that the identity of the dispreferred samples matters less than the supervision signal itself. A brief discussion in the main text about why using all non-preferred thoughts (the “All” strategy) was ultimately chosen—beyond generating more training pairs—would clarify this design decision.  
- In related work (Section 2), you contrast CPO with methods requiring searches at inference time. It would be helpful to mention Graph-of-Thoughts [9] more explicitly as another non-linear inference approach and discuss whether CPO could similarly absorb graph-based preferences.  
- The LCP gradient cancellation analysis in Section 6 is both theoretically and empirically compelling. Highlighting this contribution more prominently in the introduction—perhaps as a second bullet—would better signal its importance to readers.  
- To fully support reproducibility, consider releasing the prompt templates and the code for dataset generation alongside the inference and fine-tuning scripts. Even anonymized appendices at submission time would help reviewers understand the evaluation process.  

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
The authors provide a thoughtful discussion in Section 7, noting that CPO can be used without human annotation but could also be abused to steer models toward malicious reasoning patterns. They also mention plans to accelerate ToT data generation via non-autoregressive decoding and KV cache pruning. An explicit estimate of how much acceleration these techniques may provide would be beneficial.

Decision: 6 (Weak Accept)  
While the core idea of harnessing per-step preference signals from ToT is novel and well validated, addressing the above concerns—especially the inclusion of stronger self-improvement baselines and more comprehensive prompt documentation—would significantly strengthen the paper’s empirical foundation and reproducibility.

CPO aligns each step of CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT by leveraging preference information in the tree-search process, resulting in similar or better performance than CoT alone while avoiding the significant inference burden. Experimental results demonstrate its effectiveness.

Strengths:

Writing quality:  Your paper is well-written, with clear and precise language and a smooth flow of ideas.  The structure is reasonable,  and the logic is sound, making it very enjoyable to read.

Experimental analysis: This paper has made a full experimental demonstration.

Weaknesses:
1.  The experiment lacked a stronger baseline comparison.  In my opinion, the author's method belongs to the self-imporvement methods, so it should be compared with such typical methods, such as the ReST and self-rewarding mentioned by the author in related work.

2.  The ToT approach to dataset construction appears to be computationally expensive.

3.  the author does not seem to provide a task-specific prompt templates (although the general guideline prompt is mentioned in line 141).  This is not necessary, but I think the quality of building a preference dataset can be seriously affected by the prompt template of evaluation.

Questions:
