reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors propose an adversarial attack method that can contaminate downstream tasks from the perspective of adversarial transferability. They address the problem that SAM models do not have similar optimisation routes after fine-tuning for different downstream tasks by designing universal meta initialization. In this paper, the authors address the problem that SAM models do not have similar optimisation routes after fine-tuning for different downstream tasks by designing UMI noise. The authors introduce the idea of meta-learning to allow their algorithm to quickly adapt to different situations, i.e., downstream tasks.
reviews.strengths:
1. The theoretical part of this paper is detailed, the experiments are sufficient. The comparison with other methods shows the sophistication of their approach.



2. The attacks proposed in this paper are novel. It contributes to the topic of attacking downstream tasks of large models. A discussion on adversarial transferability is introduced under this topic.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The readability of the Methodology section of this article is somewhat poor. The authors define the problem to be solved through the form of propositions. Similarly, if the authors could summarise the formulas as Theorem and put the proof process (both formulas and reasoning) specifically in the supplementary material, it would make the article more coherent.



2. The randomness of the experimental results is unknown. I understand that due to the larger computational effort, it is not practical to report error lines on all major experiments. But it would be better for the authors to report a set of randomness on a smaller dataset and simpler settings, which will influence the reviewers' opinion of the results of this method.
reviews.questions: Two questions listed, see Weaknesses for details. Note that if the authors can demonstrate the randomness of their algorithms, that will help to get a higher rating.
reviews.limitations: The authors correctly list the implications of their work for the use of large models such as SAM in downstream tasks.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors propose an adversarial attack method that can contaminate downstream tasks from the perspective of adversarial transferability. They address the problem that SAM models do not have similar optimisation routes after fine-tuning for different downstream tasks by designing universal meta initialization. In this paper, the authors address the problem that SAM models do not have similar optimisation routes after fine-tuning for different downstream tasks by designing UMI noise. The authors introduce the idea of meta-learning to allow their algorithm to quickly adapt to different situations, i.e., downstream tasks.

Strengths:
1. The theoretical part of this paper is detailed, the experiments are sufficient. The comparison with other methods shows the sophistication of their approach.



2. The attacks proposed in this paper are novel. It contributes to the topic of attacking downstream tasks of large models. A discussion on adversarial transferability is introduced under this topic.

Weaknesses:
1. The readability of the Methodology section of this article is somewhat poor. The authors define the problem to be solved through the form of propositions. Similarly, if the authors could summarise the formulas as Theorem and put the proof process (both formulas and reasoning) specifically in the supplementary material, it would make the article more coherent.



2. The randomness of the experimental results is unknown. I understand that due to the larger computational effort, it is not practical to report error lines on all major experiments. But it would be better for the authors to report a set of randomness on a smaller dataset and simpler settings, which will influence the reviewers' opinion of the results of this method.

Questions:
Two questions listed, see Weaknesses for details. Note that if the authors can demonstrate the randomness of their algorithms, that will help to get a higher rating.

Limitations:
The authors correctly list the implications of their work for the use of large models such as SAM in downstream tasks.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
Expanded Review:

Summary:
This paper presents a novel transfer-based black-box adversarial attack framework, UMI-GRAT, that targets the Segment Anything Model (SAM) and its fine-tuned downstream segmentation models without any access to their private datasets or task-specific loss functions. Building upon the observation that fine-tuning SAM on diverse tasks (e.g., medical CT-scan segmentation, shadow detection, camouflaged object segmentation) significantly alters its optimization trajectory, the authors propose two key components: (1) Universal Meta-Initialization (UMI), an offline meta-learning procedure (see Section 4.2 and Algorithm 1) that learns an input-agnostic perturbation δ by optimizing both a universal attack objective (Equation 8) and a rapid-adaptation objective (Equation 11) via a first-order Reptile-style update (Equation 12), and (2) Gradient Robust Adversarial Loss (L_GR, Equation 18), which simulates gradient-update uncertainties through isotropic Gaussian noise augmentation on intermediate feature gradients (Proposition 1, Equation 15) to mitigate the deviation between surrogate and victim model updates. Extensive experiments on medical SAM [61], Shadow-SAM [11], and Camouflaged-SAM [11] across five datasets demonstrate that UMI-GRAT achieves substantially larger performance degradation—e.g., reducing mDSC from 81.88 to 5.22 in CT-scan segmentation—than state-of-the-art transfer attacks (MI-FGSM [13], DMI-FGSM [56], PGN [17], BSR [50], ILPD [34]). Moreover, UMI-GRAT acts as a plug-and-play enhancer when combined with existing attacks, consistently boosting their transferability (Table 1). The paper also provides theoretical insights into why a direct feature-distance maximization on the surrogate SAM leads to suboptimal adversarial updates for fine-tuned models, motivating the proposed gradient robust loss.

Strengths:
1. Detailed theoretical development. The authors carefully derive the deviation in adversarial update directions between the open-sourced SAM and its fine-tuned variants (Proposition 1, Section 4.3), and clearly motivate the need for a gradient-robust loss. The formulation of UMI via dual objectives (Equations 8 and 11) is well justified, and the meta-learning update (Equation 12) is sound. Such rigorous theoretical underpinnings lend strong credibility to the proposed method and go beyond typical heuristic-driven transfer attacks.
2. Extensive and convincing experiments. The evaluation covers three distinct downstream tasks with varying domain gaps—from highly domain-shifted medical images (synapse CT scans) to natural-image camouflage tasks. The use of multiple metrics (mDSC/mHD for medical, BER for shadow, Sα/MAE for camouflage) provides a comprehensive view of attack success. Ablations in Table 2 isolate the individual contributions of UMI and the gradient robust loss, showing that both components are necessary for peak performance. Moreover, the black-box transfer to different SAM backbones (Vit-B/L/H) reported in Appendix A.2 further validates generality.
3. Novel attack scenario and methodological contribution. Attacking downstream models purely via an open-source foundation model surrogate without knowledge of downstream data or prompts is a highly practical and underexplored threat scenario. The universal meta-initialization idea is innovative, drawing inspiration from universal perturbations [40] but instantiating it through meta-learning.

Weaknesses:
1. Readability of Methodology section. While the mathematical depth is appreciated, the dense presentation of multiple definitions (Definitions 1–2), Propositions, and multi-step equations (e.g., Equations 7–18) can overwhelm readers. The flow would benefit from summarizing the two main theoretical results as clearly stated theorems in the main text, followed by concise proof sketches, with full proofs relocated to the supplementary material. For instance, Theorem 1 could encapsulate the meta-initialization guarantee (combining Equations 8 and 11), and Theorem 2 could formalize the gradient deviation result (Equation 15). A diagram illustrating the meta-learning update loop (in addition to Figure 2) might also help guide readers through the offline UMI training.
2. Randomness and statistical variability. The paper does not report error bars or confidence intervals for the main attack success metrics. Although computationally heavy, even a small-scale randomness study—such as evaluating mDSC/mHD over multiple runs with different seeds on a subset of CT-scan images—would bolster confidence in the stability of UMI-GRAT. The authors could, for instance, follow the approach outlined in Appendix A.1 for a representative subset, reporting standard deviations alongside the mean values. This will help reviewers and practitioners gauge the reproducibility of results.

Suggestions:
– To improve clarity, include a high-level algorithmic summary box or pseudocode for UMI-GRAT that emphasizes the two stages (offline UMI learning and real-time GR attack) and highlights parameter settings (Tm=7, η=1, Ta=10, α=2, ϵ=10) used in experiments.
– Add a lightweight discussion or visualization of how the learned δ from UMI evolves across meta-iterations—e.g., show sample perturbations on natural images after t=1, 3, 7 meta-steps—to give readers intuition about the universal vulnerability being captured.
– Consider adding a short paragraph in the main text summarizing the randomness study from Appendix A.1 and indicating that UMI-GRAT exhibits comparable variability to baseline attacks.

Questions for the authors:
1. How sensitive is UMI-GRAT to the choice of the natural image dataset D used for meta-training? Would replacing ImageNet + SA-1B with a smaller or more domain-restricted dataset significantly impact transferability?
2. In scenarios where downstream fine-tuning is limited to adapter modules rather than full encoder updates, do you anticipate the gradient deviation (Proposition 1) to be smaller, possibly reducing the relative benefit of L_GR?

Limitations (as noted in the paper):
– The current evaluation focuses exclusively on SAM and its variants. It remains to be seen how UMI-GRAT generalizes to other foundation models (e.g., ViT-based classification backbones or vision-language encoders).
– While the paper highlights adversarial vulnerabilities, it does not propose corresponding defense mechanisms. Incorporating an initial study of possible defenses (e.g., adversarial fine-tuning, gradient masking) could anchor the security implications more fully.

Decision:
This work addresses an important and timely security challenge for large foundation models, delivers a novel meta-learning-based attack, and supports its claims with strong theoretical and empirical evidence. I recommend acceptance (rating 5).

Reviewer Rating: 5

reviews.summary: This paper investigates the vulnerability of Segment Anything Model (SAM) and its downstream models to transferable adversarial attacks. The authors propose a novel attack method called Universal Meta-Initialized and Gradient Robust Adversarial attack (UMI-GRAT) that leverages the open-sourced SAM to generate adversarial examples effective against fine-tuned downstream models, even without access to the downstream task or dataset.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper tackles a practical and challenging problem of attacking downstream models fine-tuned from a publicly available foundation model without knowledge of the downstream task or data.
2. The proposed UMI-GRAT method is well-motivated and technically sound. The authors provide theoretical insights into the gradient deviation problem and propose a robust solution using gradient noise augmentation.
3. The paper presents extensive experiments demonstrating the effectiveness of UMI-GRAT in attacking SAM and its downstream models
reviews.weaknesses: See Questions.
reviews.questions:
1. How does the performance of UMI-GRAT vary with different choices of hyperparameters, such as the perturbation bound ε and the number of iterations in UMI and LGR. Especially, line 278 mentions that the perturbation bound is 10, which is a bit too large.
2. According to the latest benchmark **[R1]**, baselines used in the paper are not SOTA methods. How does it compare with NCS **[R2]**, ANDA **[R3]**, DeCowA **[R4]** and L2T **[R5]**?
3. Is the proposed method a universal transfer attack method? Although this question is mentioned on line 548, can the performance of UMI-GRAT and SOTA be compared under a general transfer attack test setting?
4. UMI-GRAT is a gradient-based attack method. How does the proposed method perform when the model has a certain robustness (such as adversarial training)?

---
**[R1]** Devling into Adversarial Transferability on Image Classification: A Review, Benchmark and Evaluation.   
**[R2]** Enhancing Adversarial Transferability Through Neighborhood Conditional Sampling.   
**[R3]** Strong Transferable Adversarial Attacks via Ensembled Asymptotically Normal Distribution Learning. CVPR. 2024.   
**[R4]** Boosting Adversarial Transferability across Model Genus by Deformation-Constrained Warping.   
**[R5]** Learning to Transform Dynamically for Better Adversarial Transferability
reviews.limitations: Limitations are discussed in the Appendix.

Summary:
This paper investigates the vulnerability of Segment Anything Model (SAM) and its downstream models to transferable adversarial attacks. The authors propose a novel attack method called Universal Meta-Initialized and Gradient Robust Adversarial attack (UMI-GRAT) that leverages the open-sourced SAM to generate adversarial examples effective against fine-tuned downstream models, even without access to the downstream task or dataset.

Strengths:
1. The paper tackles a practical and challenging problem of attacking downstream models fine-tuned from a publicly available foundation model without knowledge of the downstream task or data.
2. The proposed UMI-GRAT method is well-motivated and technically sound. The authors provide theoretical insights into the gradient deviation problem and propose a robust solution using gradient noise augmentation.
3. The paper presents extensive experiments demonstrating the effectiveness of UMI-GRAT in attacking SAM and its downstream models

Weaknesses:
See Questions.

Questions:
1. How does the performance of UMI-GRAT vary with different choices of hyperparameters, such as the perturbation bound ε and the number of iterations in UMI and LGR. Especially, line 278 mentions that the perturbation bound is 10, which is a bit too large.
2. According to the latest benchmark **[R1]**, baselines used in the paper are not SOTA methods. How does it compare with NCS **[R2]**, ANDA **[R3]**, DeCowA **[R4]** and L2T **[R5]**?
3. Is the proposed method a universal transfer attack method? Although this question is mentioned on line 548, can the performance of UMI-GRAT and SOTA be compared under a general transfer attack test setting?
4. UMI-GRAT is a gradient-based attack method. How does the proposed method perform when the model has a certain robustness (such as adversarial training)?

---
**[R1]** Devling into Adversarial Transferability on Image Classification: A Review, Benchmark and Evaluation.   
**[R2]** Enhancing Adversarial Transferability Through Neighborhood Conditional Sampling.   
**[R3]** Strong Transferable Adversarial Attacks via Ensembled Asymptotically Normal Distribution Learning. CVPR. 2024.   
**[R4]** Boosting Adversarial Transferability across Model Genus by Deformation-Constrained Warping.   
**[R5]** Learning to Transform Dynamically for Better Adversarial Transferability

Limitations:
Limitations are discussed in the Appendix.

Rating:
5

title: Follow Hamiltonian Leader: An Efficient Energy-Guided Sampling Method

reviews.summary:
This paper presents an interesting new approach for improving sampling methods for energy-based generative models and score-matching models. The key idea is to incorporate zeroth-order information (energy values) in addition to the typical first-order gradient information used by most sampling algorithms like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC).

The authors identify several challenging scenarios where relying solely on gradients can be problematic - cases of instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability. They argue that incorporating energy values can help mitigate issues in these situations and improve sampling efficiency and quality.

Overall, the core idea of leveraging zeroth-order information in addition to gradients is quite novel and the FHL algorithm is an elegant way to implement this for improving sampling efficiency and quality. The paper is well-motivated, the method is clearly explained, and the empirical results are compelling.
reviews.strengths:
1. Novel idea of incorporating zeroth-order energy information into sampling algorithms like HMC, which typically only use gradients. This can help address issues like instability and metastability.

2. The Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) algorithm is an elegant way to exchange both energy and gradient information across parallel sampling chains in a principled manner.

3. Thorough experimental evaluation across synthetic examples illustrating the identified challenging scenarios of instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability.

4. Promising results showing improved sampling quality over baselines for energy-based generative models on real datasets like CLEVR.

5. Clear motivation and well-explained methodology.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. It would be better to show exploration of the sensitivity to key hyperparameters like the number of parallel sampling chains.

2. Discussion of computational cost/overhead compared to baseline sampling methods are missing in the manuscript.
reviews.questions:
1. While the empirical results are compelling, can you provide any theoretical analysis or guarantees about the convergence properties, sampling quality, or stationary distribution of the FHL algorithm? Even approximate bounds or insights would strengthen the theoretical grounding.

2. What are the main computational and memory overheads introduced by running parallel chains in FHL compared to standard HMC? Is there a limit on scalability to high dimensions or is there a way to reduce overhead through approximations?
reviews.limitations: Based on the provided paper, the authors do not appear to have explicitly discussed the limitations or potential negative societal impacts of their work. The paper is primarily focused on presenting the technical details of the proposed Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) sampling algorithm and its empirical evaluation.

Summary:
This paper presents an interesting new approach for improving sampling methods for energy-based generative models and score-matching models. The key idea is to incorporate zeroth-order information (energy values) in addition to the typical first-order gradient information used by most sampling algorithms like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC).

The authors identify several challenging scenarios where relying solely on gradients can be problematic - cases of instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability. They argue that incorporating energy values can help mitigate issues in these situations and improve sampling efficiency and quality.

Overall, the core idea of leveraging zeroth-order information in addition to gradients is quite novel and the FHL algorithm is an elegant way to implement this for improving sampling efficiency and quality. The paper is well-motivated, the method is clearly explained, and the empirical results are compelling.

Strengths:
1. Novel idea of incorporating zeroth-order energy information into sampling algorithms like HMC, which typically only use gradients. This can help address issues like instability and metastability.

2. The Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) algorithm is an elegant way to exchange both energy and gradient information across parallel sampling chains in a principled manner.

3. Thorough experimental evaluation across synthetic examples illustrating the identified challenging scenarios of instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability.

4. Promising results showing improved sampling quality over baselines for energy-based generative models on real datasets like CLEVR.

5. Clear motivation and well-explained methodology.

Weaknesses:
1. It would be better to show exploration of the sensitivity to key hyperparameters like the number of parallel sampling chains.

2. Discussion of computational cost/overhead compared to baseline sampling methods are missing in the manuscript.

Questions:
1. While the empirical results are compelling, can you provide any theoretical analysis or guarantees about the convergence properties, sampling quality, or stationary distribution of the FHL algorithm? Even approximate bounds or insights would strengthen the theoretical grounding.

2. What are the main computational and memory overheads introduced by running parallel chains in FHL compared to standard HMC? Is there a limit on scalability to high dimensions or is there a way to reduce overhead through approximations?

Limitations:
Based on the provided paper, the authors do not appear to have explicitly discussed the limitations or potential negative societal impacts of their work. The paper is primarily focused on presenting the technical details of the proposed Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) sampling algorithm and its empirical evaluation.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
Expanded Review:

Summary:  
This manuscript introduces a compelling new sampling framework—Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL)—that augments traditional Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) style samplers by integrating zeroth-order energy values alongside first-order gradients. The authors build on score-based generative models and energy-based models (EBMs) and argue that, in practice, gradients can be unreliable or misleading under three adversarial conditions: instability (ill-conditioned Hessians), metastability (chains trapped in local minima), and pseudo-stability (misleading gradient directions). To address these issues, FHL runs multiple parallel chains (grouped in size n) and dynamically designates the lowest-energy chain as “leader.” Through an Elastic Leapfrog integrator (Algorithm 1) that adds an elastic potential E(x; x_l) = (ρ/2) ‖x – x_l‖², and a joint Metropolis‐Hastings step across all chains (Algorithm 2), FHL exchanges both energy and gradient information to steer exploration toward high-density regions. The paper demonstrates in synthetic Gaussian and mixture-model settings (Figures 4–6) that FHL more rapidly converges to true modes than baselines like LMC, unadjusted Langevin (U-LMC), HMC, and U-HMC. Further, on joint energy classifiers (JEM) and Clevr compositional score‐matching experiments, FHL yields superior sample quality with crisper object shapes and fewer artifacts (Figures 7, 9, 10). Overall, the paper makes a strong case that leveraging zeroth-order energy can substantially improve sampling efficiency and robustness.

Strengths:  
1. Novelty of zeroth-order energy integration.  Incorporating energy values directly into the proposal mechanism is an original contribution, especially as most samplers rely exclusively on ∇U(x). By weighting pulls toward the energy-based leader (ρ_i ∝ π_l – π_i), FHL addresses gradient corruption and landscape ill-conditioning in a principled way.  
2. Elegant algorithm design.  The Elastic Leapfrog step is simple to implement—just two extra gradient terms per chain—and the leader-pulling mechanism naturally fits into existing HMC frameworks. The authors clearly describe how to compute ρ_i in Equation (5) and implement acceptance using the joint canonical distribution.  
3. Thorough synthetic evaluation.  The paper includes carefully designed experiments for instability (high‐conditioned 2D Gaussian), metastability (zero gradients leading to random walks; Figure 5), and pseudo-stability (compositional target distributions with misleading ∇ log Q; Figure 6). These illustrate exactly the failure modes that FHL overcomes.  
4. Real-world applicability.  On Clevr compositional generation, FHL cleanly composes sphere and cylinder models without introducing extraneous cubes, outperforming MALA and standard HMC. In the classifier-as-energy setting, FHL produces sharper CIFAR images (Figure 7), demonstrating real-data impact.  
5. Clear motivation and presentation.  The authors motivate each design choice—parallel tempering inspiration, eLeapfrog spring interpretation—and provide pseudocode (Algorithms 1–2) and a supplementary theorem bounding the bias when the leader is corrupted. The writing is concise yet detailed.

Weaknesses:  
1. Hyperparameter sensitivity.  The paper does not explore how performance varies with the number of chains n, pulling strength λ, or step size η. An ablation study varying n∈{2,4,8} or λ∈[0,1] would clarify the robustness of FHL’s gains.  
2. Computational and memory overhead.  Running n parallel HMC chains with elastic coupling incurs extra gradient computations (n times cost) and requires broadcasting the leader state each step. The manuscript does not discuss wall-clock time comparisons or GPU memory footprint relative to single-chain HMC.

Questions for the Authors:  
1. Can you provide theoretical guarantees on detailed balance and convergence?  Aside from the supplementary theorem on Wasserstein bias, it would strengthen the work to outline conditions under which FHL’s Markov chain remains ergodic and converges to π(x). Are there mixing time bounds or spectral gap estimates?  
2. What is the increase in computational and memory cost?  For a given budget of gradient evaluations, how does FHL’s effective sample size compare to standard HMC? Can you suggest approximations—e.g., sparse leader updates or asynchronous coupling—that reduce overhead for high-dimensional problems?

Limitations:  
The manuscript does not explicitly discuss potential failure modes—for instance, if the designated leader reports artificially low energy due to model mis-specification, chains could be biased toward incorrect regions. A brief discussion of such limitations and possible guardrails (e.g., periodic re-initialization or annealing λ→0) would be valuable. The paper also omits any negative societal impacts or broader ethical considerations, though the technical focus is narrow.

Overall, this work presents a well-motivated, novel algorithm with strong empirical evidence of improved sampling under challenging scenarios. Addressing the above questions on theoretical grounding and computational overhead would further solidify its contributions. I therefore recommend acceptance (rating 7).

reviews.summary: This work proposes to incorporate the energy $U$ into the gradient-based sampling techniques. In particular, it proposes to choose the lowest energy particle as the leader and then add an extra elastic tension between the leader and followers in the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo method.
reviews.strengths: The idea is simple and clear, the toy examples are easy to understand and demonstrate the benefit of the proposed method well. In addition, the authors conduct experiments for each of the three challenging sampling scenarios identified by the authors.
reviews.weaknesses:
It might worth including the overhead of the proposed method, how much slower the algorithm is per iteration compared to HMC for instance.

The tension coefficient $\lambda$ is critical, setting it to 0 recovers the baseline. But I did not find an ablation over the $\lambda$, is it hard to choose? From my understanding, if you set $\lambda$ pretty large it might recover something like gradient descent and the sampling will collapse.
reviews.questions:
1. How is $\lambda$ determined per experiment? Is it possible to include an ablation over it for the three scenarios at least?

2. In Figure 7, it is unclear to me which method is better. It will be better to provide some quantitative measurement like FID or IS scores?
reviews.limitations: Yes.

reviews.text:
Expanded Review

Summary:  
This manuscript introduces a novel enhancement to gradient-based sampling techniques by incorporating the zeroth-order energy function \(U(x)\) directly into the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) framework. The key idea is to run multiple parallel chains, designate the lowest-energy sample as the “leader” at each iteration, and apply an elastic coupling force between this leader and the other “follower” particles. The modified leapfrog integrator (eLeapfrog, Algorithm 1) and the full Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL, Algorithm 2) procedure are clearly described. Toy problems involving instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability illustrate that FHL more rapidly converges to high-probability modes compared to standard HMC, Langevin Monte Carlo (LMC), and their unadjusted variants (U-HMC and U-LMC). Additional experiments on energy-based generative models—including conditional sampling with a pre-trained WideResNet classifier on ImageNet classes and score-matching diffusion models—further demonstrate FHL’s improved sample quality and robustness to corrupted gradients.

Strengths:  
1. Clarity and simplicity of the core idea. The manuscript's description of leader-pulling (Section 4.2) and elastic leapfrog integration (Section 4.1) is concise, making the algorithm easy to implement and reason about. Figure 1–3 visually reinforce how an anchor leader stabilizes sampling under gradient disturbances, escapes local minima, and avoids pseudo-stable traps.  
2. Well-designed motivating examples. The three adversarial scenarios—instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability—are exactly the kinds of practical difficulties encountered in high-dimensional energy-based models (Section 3). The 2D Gaussian sampling experiments (Figures 4–6) and CLEVR toy tasks (Figures 9–10) effectively highlight FHL’s advantages in each case.  
3. Thorough algorithmic presentation. The pseudocode in Algorithm 1 (eLeapfrog) and Algorithm 2 (FHL) is detailed, specifying inputs such as learning rate η, pulling strength λ, and the acceptance criterion. The supplementary material (Section A) even connects leader-pulling to optimization analogues like simulated annealing and heavy-ball methods.  
4. Empirical validation on diverse tasks. Beyond toy distributions, the authors evaluate FHL on energy-guided conditional sampling with a pre-trained classifier (Section 5.2, Figure 7) and on compositional diffusion models (Section 5.3, Figures 8 and 16–20). These experiments show FHL yields sharper generated images and more accurate compositional sampling.

Weaknesses:  
1. Runtime overhead per iteration is not reported. Introducing elastic forces and leader selection among \(n\) particles (e.g., \(n=4\) in experiments) inevitably incurs additional computation compared to a single-chain HMC. A wall-clock comparison or flop count per iteration versus standard HMC/LMC would clarify the cost–benefit trade-off. For instance, evaluating \(\rho_i\) and updating pairwise distances \(\|x_i - x_\ell\|\) in each leapfrog step adds \(O(n\,d)\) work—how does this scale in practice?  
2. Sensitivity to the pulling strength \(\lambda\). The manuscript notes that \(\lambda=0\) recovers vanilla HMC, while large \(\lambda\) could over-couple the particles, reducing exploration. However, no systematic study of \(\lambda\) values is presented. An ablation plot showing performance (e.g., convergence error or sample quality) versus \(\lambda\) in each of the three adversarial scenarios would guide practitioners in selecting this critical hyperparameter. Without such guidance, it is unclear how robust the method is to mis-specification of \(\lambda\).

Suggestions for Improvement:  
• Include an ablation study over \(\lambda\). For example, in the 2D Gaussian instability experiment (Figure 4), vary \(\lambda\in\{0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0\}\) and plot the average energy of the best particle after \(T=1000\) steps.  
• Report per-iteration wall-clock time or flop counts. A table comparing single-step runtime of HMC vs. FHL (with \(n\) particles) on a standard benchmark (e.g., 2D Gaussian, ResNet-based EBM sampler) would quantify the computational overhead.  
• Provide quantitative sample-quality metrics in Figure 7. While the conditional sampling images for ImageNet classes (planes, birds, frogs, dogs) visually appear sharper under FHL, metrics such as FID or Inception Score would substantiate the qualitative observation.

Questions:  
1. How is \(\lambda\) determined in each experiment? Is it selected via grid search on a validation set, or set heuristically based on problem scale?  
2. In Figure 7 (conditional classifier-guided sampling), can the authors report FID/IS scores or log-likelihood improvements to quantify which method performs better? Visual comparisons alone may be subject to perception bias.

Limitations:  
The authors acknowledge that integrating energy information comes at the cost of extra computations (e.g., computing elastic energy and leader selection). Additionally, the choice of \(\lambda\) can significantly influence sampling behavior, potentially requiring problem-specific tuning.

Rating: 6  

Overall, this paper proposes a straightforward and well-motivated extension to HMC that consistently improves convergence and sample quality across several challenging settings. Addressing the overhead and hyperparameter-sensitivity concerns will further strengthen the practical impact of this work. Given the clarity of the idea, the detailed algorithmic exposition, and the demonstrable empirical gains, I recommend acceptance with minor revisions.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a novel parallel sampling method named "Follow Hamiltonian Leader" (FHL) designed to address sampling challenges by leveraging zeroth-order information, particularly when first-order data is unreliable or unavailable. The method incorporates a leader-guiding mechanism to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of the sampling process. Experimental results indicate that FHL significantly improves the exploration of target distributions and outperforms traditional sampling techniques, especially in scenarios involving corrupted gradients.
reviews.strengths:
1. Innovative combination of zeroth and first-order information.
2. The effectiveness of the method is demonstrated in multiple task scenarios.
3. Theoretical analysis and prove are sufficient.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. Is there any quantitative experiments like evaluating FID and IS on cifar10 datasets and I think it's more compelling whether a novel sampling methods combined with generative models can be used on image datasets with more complex distributions.
2. Lack of experiment of OOD in combination with EBMs or score-based models to valid the stability during sampling with proposed method.

This paper presents a novel parallel sampling method named "Follow Hamiltonian Leader" (FHL) designed to address sampling challenges by leveraging zeroth-order information, particularly when first-order data is unreliable or unavailable. Rating:
6
reviews.text:
Expanded Review

Summary:  
This paper introduces Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL), a novel parallel sampling algorithm that integrates both zeroth-order (energy) information and first-order (gradient) data to improve exploration in challenging sampling scenarios—especially when gradient information is corrupted or unreliable. FHL draws inspiration from parallel tempering and leader–follower dynamics: multiple HMC chains run in parallel, grouped into sets of n=4 particles, and at each leapfrog iteration (via the proposed Elastic Leapfrog integrator) particles are elastically “pulled” toward the lowest-energy chain (the leader). The approach maintains detailed balance through a group-level Metropolis acceptance test (Algorithm 2). Empirical results on toy Gaussian mixtures (Figures 4–6), energy-based generative models (CLEVR, Figure 9–10), and compositional score-based models (Section 5.3.1 synthetic, Section 5.3.2 CLEVR) show that FHL converges faster, escapes metastable and pseudo-stable traps, and produces higher-quality samples than baselines (LMC, U-LMC, HMC, U-HMC).

Strengths:  
1. Innovative integration of zeroth-order energy guidance with traditional gradient-driven HMC. By incorporating an elastic energy term E(x; x̃)=½‖x–x̃‖² in the Hamiltonian (Equation 4), FHL dynamically transfers “spring” energy from the leader to explorers, boosting their ability to cross energy barriers in the instability and metastability cases.  
2. Thorough empirical evaluation across three adversarial scenarios—instability (ill-conditioned Gaussians, Figure 4), metastability (flat gradients, Figure 5), and pseudo-stability (misleading gradients from ∇ log Q vs. true energy π, Figure 6)—demonstrating consistent improvements in mode discovery rates and sample fidelity. The toy experiments use a ResNet-based Uθ energy model (6-layer, 256 hidden units) and step sizes η in {0.002, 0.0002, 0.005, 0.0005}, providing a clear comparative framework.  
3. Theoretical underpinning and proof (Supplement, Theorem 1) show that sampling bias introduced by the elastic pulling term can be controlled (Wasserstein distance bound vanishes as λ→0), adding rigor to the algorithmic design.  

Weaknesses:  
1. Limited demonstration on high-dimensional, complex datasets. While the paper convincingly applies FHL to two-dimensional toy mixtures and CLEVR primitives, it lacks quantitative evaluations (e.g., FID, IS) on standard image benchmarks such as CIFAR-10. Incorporating such metrics would strengthen the claim that FHL scales to real-world generative modeling tasks.  
2. The paper omits out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness experiments for energy-based models. In Section 5.2 and 5.3, the focus remains on in-distribution sampling quality; however, demonstrating how FHL copes with OOD inputs (or sampling stability across varying classifier confidences) would clarify its practical applicability in EBMs.  

Questions for the authors:  
– Why is the extra elastic energy term chosen as the squared Euclidean distance E(x, x̃)=½‖x–x̃‖²? Could other norms or divergence measures (e.g., Mahalanobis distance aligned with local curvature) further improve exploration?  
– Is there a connection between your elastic pulling mechanism and the local-entropy regularization in Entropy-SGD [Chaudhari et al. 2017]? Both methods bias exploration toward wide, flat regions—could insights from that work inform hyperparameter choices for λ?  

Limitations (as noted by the authors):  
1. The integration of FHL with other advanced MCMC variants—such as NUTS, Riemann manifold HMC, or stochastic-gradient HMC—remains unexplored. Such combinations could further improve adaptability in high-dimensional, non-stationary models.  
2. Quantitative comparisons against competitive sampling algorithms (beyond GAN-based metrics) are missing. Adding evaluation on likelihood-estimation tasks or classification-driven sampling performance would provide a more comprehensive assessment of FHL’s advantages.

Rating: 6  
The paper presents a compelling and well-motivated algorithm with solid theoretical and empirical support. However, broader experiments on complex, high-dimensional datasets and deeper quantitative analysis (e.g., FID/IS scores, OOD robustness) are needed to fully validate the method’s impact in realistic generative modeling contexts.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces an interesting parallel sampling method that leverages zeroth-order information to address challenges in sampling from probability distributions, particularly when first-order data is unreliable or unavailable. The method incorporates a leader-guiding mechanism, enhancing efficiency and effectiveness by connecting multiple sampling instances through a selected leader. The proposed method, named Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL), extends the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) framework by concurrently running multiple replicas at different energy levels and combining both zeroth and first-order information from various chains. Experimental results demonstrate that FHL significantly improves the exploration of target distributions and produces higher-quality outcomes compared to traditional sampling techniques, showing resilience against corrupted gradients and excelling in scenarios characterized by instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability.
reviews.strengths:
- The proposed Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) method markedly improves the efficiency and effectiveness of sampling processes, significantly expediting the exploration of target distributions and producing superior quality outcomes compared to traditional sampling techniques.
- FHL demonstrates greater resilience against the detrimental impacts of corrupted gradients by incorporating zeroth-order information. This robustness makes the method particularly valuable in scenarios where first-order information is compromised, ensuring more reliable and accurate sampling.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The proposed FHL method involves intricate modifications to the traditional Hamiltonian Monte Carlo framework, such as the leader-guiding mechanism and elastic leapfrog technique, which may increase the complexity of implementation and require significant computational resources.
- The effectiveness of the FHL method heavily relies on the appropriate selection of the leader particle. If the leader is not accurately chosen, it could lead to suboptimal sampling performance, potentially compromising the overall efficiency and accuracy of the method.
- While the paper presents experimental results to demonstrate the efficacy of the FHL method, there is a lack of in-depth theoretical analysis to rigorously establish the convergence properties and performance guarantees of the proposed approach.
- The method’s scalability to high-dimensional problems or extremely large datasets is not thoroughly addressed. The parallel sampling approach may encounter challenges in maintaining efficiency and effectiveness as the dimensionality and size of the data increase.
reviews.questions: See weaknesses above.
reviews.limitations: The authors have not adequately addressed the limitations.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces an interesting parallel sampling method that leverages zeroth-order information to address challenges in sampling from probability distributions, particularly when first-order data is unreliable or unavailable. The method incorporates a leader-guiding mechanism, enhancing efficiency and effectiveness by connecting multiple sampling instances through a selected leader. The proposed method, named Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL), extends the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) framework by concurrently running multiple replicas at different energy levels and combining both zeroth and first-order information from various chains. Experimental results demonstrate that FHL significantly improves the exploration of target distributions and produces higher-quality outcomes compared to traditional sampling techniques, showing resilience against corrupted gradients and excelling in scenarios characterized by instability, metastability, and pseudo-stability.

Strengths:
- The proposed Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL) method markedly improves the efficiency and effectiveness of sampling processes, significantly expediting the exploration of target distributions and producing superior quality outcomes compared to traditional sampling techniques.
- FHL demonstrates greater resilience against the detrimental impacts of corrupted gradients by incorporating zeroth-order information. This robustness makes the method particularly valuable in scenarios where first-order information is compromised, ensuring more reliable and accurate sampling.

Weaknesses:
- The proposed FHL method involves intricate modifications to the traditional Hamiltonian Monte Carlo framework, such as the leader-guiding mechanism and elastic leapfrog technique, which may increase the complexity of implementation and require significant computational resources.
- The effectiveness of the FHL method heavily relies on the appropriate selection of the leader particle. If the leader is not accurately chosen, it could lead to suboptimal sampling performance, potentially compromising the overall efficiency and accuracy of the method.
- While the paper presents experimental results to demonstrate the efficacy of the FHL method, there is a lack of in-depth theoretical analysis to rigorously establish the convergence properties and performance guarantees of the proposed approach.
- The method’s scalability to high-dimensional problems or extremely large datasets is not thoroughly addressed. The parallel sampling approach may encounter challenges in maintaining efficiency and effectiveness as the dimensionality and size of the data increase.

Questions:
See weaknesses above.

Limitations:
The authors have not adequately addressed the limitations.

Rating:
4
reviews.text:
Expanded Review:

Summary:  
This manuscript presents Follow Hamiltonian Leader (FHL), a novel parallel sampling framework built on Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) that integrates zeroth-order energy information with first-order gradient data. The core idea is to run multiple HMC replicas in parallel, group them (e.g., into sets of four particles), and identify a “leader” in each group based on the lowest energy U(x). The algorithm’s Elastic Leapfrog (eLeapfrog) integrator adds an elastic force term that pulls non‐leader particles toward the leader, enhancing local exploitation while preserving exploratory momentum. By combining both ∇U(x) and the scalar energy U(x) during the Metropolis acceptance step (Algorithm 2), FHL aims to address three common challenges in sampling: (1) instability due to ill-conditioned local Hessians, (2) metastability wherein particles get trapped in intermediate-energy basins, and (3) pseudo-stability caused by misleading or corrupted gradients. Empirical evaluations on toy Gaussian mixtures (Figures 4–6), energy-based generative models with classifier-derived guidance (Figure 7), and compositional score-matching tasks on CLEVR (Figures 9–10) demonstrate that FHL converges more rapidly to true modes, escapes traps more effectively, and produces higher-quality samples than baselines such as LMC, HMC, U-LMC, and U-HMC.

Strengths:  
• Enhanced Sampling Efficiency and Quality: FHL consistently outperforms traditional methods in all three adversarial gradient scenarios. In the instability experiments (Section 5.1.1, Figure 4), FHL’s lowest‐energy particles converge to the Gaussian mode faster and with fewer deviations than HMC or Langevin approaches. Its elastic coupling ensures that particles benefit from both local exploitation around the leader and global exploration, which accelerates mixing.  
• Robustness to Corrupted or Uninformative Gradients: By incorporating the zeroth-order term U(x) into both proposal generation and acceptance decisions, FHL remains effective even when ∇U(x) is unreliable or intentionally perturbed. In the metastability test with zero gradients (Section 5.1.2, Figure 5), FHL locates the target mode rapidly whereas U-HMC and U-LMC degrade to near-random walks. Similarly, in the pseudo-stability setting (Section 5.1.3, Figure 6), FHL escapes misleading gradient basins and correctly samples from the intended mixture components more quickly than baselines. This resilience is particularly valuable for energy-based score-matching models, where the score network may produce biased estimates.  
• Generality Across Model Classes: The authors demonstrate FHL’s applicability both on synthetic low-dimensional examples and on high-dimensional image-generation tasks. Section 5.2 shows that FHL generates sharper, higher-fidelity class-conditional images from a JEM classifier (Figures 7, 15) compared to standard HMC with equal compute budgets. In Section 5.3, FHL successfully composes independently trained EBMs on CLEVR to sample scenes with the desired combination of shapes (Figures 9–10), a task where naive score sum leads to artifact-laden results [12].  
• Integration Potential: The paper notes that FHL’s leader‐pulling scheme and eLeapfrog integrator are modular and can be combined with existing HMC enhancements such as NUTS or Riemann manifold adaptation. This flexibility suggests a broader impact on the MCMC community, potentially improving sampling in diverse applications from Bayesian inference to diffusion-based generative modeling.

Weaknesses:  
• Increased Algorithmic Complexity and Computational Overhead: The introduction of the leader-guiding mechanism and elastic coupling in eLeapfrog adds multiple O(n) operations per leapfrog step—computing energies of all n particles to select the leader and calculating elastic forces with coefficient ρ_i. This extra bookkeeping, along with the group‐level Metropolis acceptance in Algorithm 2, may substantially increase wall‐clock runtime and memory usage compared to standard HMC. Large-scale problems with hundreds or thousands of replicas could face prohibitive resource demands.  
• Sensitivity to Leader Selection: The method’s performance hinges on accurately identifying a reliable leader with the lowest energy. If the leader particle is corrupted—reporting artificially low energy or initialized far from the true mode—non‐leader particles may be unduly pulled toward a suboptimal region. While Section C in the supplement analyzes bias when the leader is biased (Theorem 1), practical strategies for mitigating poor leader choice (e.g., adaptive group resizing, outlier detection, or fallback procedures) are not fully explored.  
• Lack of Rigorous Theoretical Guarantees: Although the supplement offers a bound on the Wasserstein distance when sampling from a perturbed Boltzmann distribution, the paper does not establish formal convergence rates, mixing-time analyses, or spectral gap improvements for FHL. A more in-depth theoretical treatment—perhaps leveraging coupling arguments or log-Sobolev inequalities—would strengthen confidence in the method’s reliability across arbitrary target densities.  
• Scalability to High Dimensions and Large Datasets: The experiments, while diverse, are limited to synthetic 2D/784D Gaussian mixtures, CIFAR‐scale classifier guidance, and CLEVR scenes at modest resolution. It remains unclear how FHL scales to extremely high-dimensional latent spaces (e.g., 10⁶ parameters in state‐of‐the‐art diffusion models) or to large datasets requiring millions of sampling steps. Communication and synchronization overhead in a distributed setting could further hamper performance.

Additional Observations and Suggestions for Improvement:  
• Ablation Studies on Elastic Force and Pulling Strength λ: The manuscript uses λ values chosen empirically, but an ablation that systematically varies λ and group size n would clarify the tradeoff between exploration and exploitation. Reporting effective sample size or autocorrelation times as λ→0 versus λ→large could guide practitioners in selecting hyperparameters.  
• Hybridization with NUTS or Stochastic Gradient HMC: Demonstrating FHL’s compatibility with adaptive step-size schemes (NUTS) or stochastic-gradient variants in large‐dataset regimes would underscore its practical versatility.  
• Computational Cost Reporting: Including wall‐clock times, GPU/CPU usage, and memory footprints for each method would help readers assess the efficiency gains relative to the increased complexity.  
• Extended Theoretical Analysis: A sketch of how FHL modifies detailed balance or affects conductance in multimodal landscapes could round out the presentation and serve as a foundation for future work on convergence proofs.

Decision: 4 (Reject)  
Although Follow Hamiltonian Leader is an innovative and promising approach that effectively leverages zeroth-order information to enhance sampling robustness and quality, the increased algorithmic complexity, sensitivity to leader choice, absence of thorough theoretical guarantees, and uncertain scalability to truly large-scale settings temper enthusiasm for immediate acceptance. With additional analysis, ablations, and scalability studies, FHL could make a strong contribution, but in its current form the paper falls short of the standard required for acceptance.

title: Metric Distortion Under Probabilistic Voting

reviews.summary: Metric distortion is a framework to evaluate the "accuracy" of social choice rules, by considering a worst-case candidate and voter embedding in a metric space, and by assuming that reported votes are derived from distances in the metric space. So far, votes were assumed to be a deterministic function of the distances. The paper investigates the case where they are probabilistic functions of the distances, in the asymptotic limit of a large number of voters. The key finding is that this inverts the evaluation of some voting rules, in particular Copeland and Random Dictatorship, for highly noisy voting.
reviews.strengths:
The model introduced by the authors is an insightful generalization of previous work which, remarkably, provides a markedly different view on social choice rules. Given the growing importance of social choice in machine learning, as well as accounting for noisy inputs while considering embedded vector spaces, I believe that this work scores high in significance.

Additionally, the analysis is quite thorough, with matching lower/upper asymptotic bounds for Plurality upper bound for Copeland and lower/upper bounds for Random Dictatorship.

The paper is also fairly well written.
reviews.weaknesses:
My main concern is Lemma 3. The proof seems to argue that the constraints $\forall i, | b_i - w_i | \leq b_i + w_i$ (i.e. inequality constraint on $(i, W, B)$), but the optimization problem (7) has a constraint $\max_i | w_i - b_i | \leq \min_i b_i + w_i$. It is not clear to me why these constraints would be equivalent. Note that the latter implies the former set of constraints. Thus if $\mathcal E_{\alpha}'$ was defined with all voter-wise constraints, then it would be a minimum over a smaller set, and thus $opt(\mathcal E_\alpha') \geq opt(\mathcal E_\alpha)$. Since Lemma 3's proof actually says $\frac{SC(W, d)}{SC(B, d)} \leq 1 / opt(\mathcal E'_\alpha)$, using this inequality seems to imply the actual Lemma 3. Am I reading this correctly?

It is disappointing that the upper-bound for Copeland. It would be helpful if the authors can point out where the argument gets loose.
reviews.questions:
Could the authors clarify the proof of Lemma 3? I will happily increase my score given a convincing response.

Can the authors provide insight into why the Copeland upper bound is not tight?

More anecdotically, I wonder why "Metric Distortion" is restricted to the distortion of ranking-based algorithms. I feel that the concept is more general than that. Essentially, it is the worst social cost ratio of any voting rule, when executed on votes derived from distances in the metric space, right? In particular, it could be studied even for the social-cost minimizing geometric median, but given, e.g., noisy inputs or strategic votes? I believe that this is an interesting research venue, given that machine learning provides many tools to embed candidate features and voters' preferences in a vector space.
reviews.limitations: The paper stated its results very clearly and factually. I have no concerns about unaddressed limitations.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
Metric distortion is a framework to evaluate the "accuracy" of social choice rules, by considering a worst-case candidate and voter embedding in a metric space, and by assuming that reported votes are derived from distances in the metric space. So far, votes were assumed to be a deterministic function of the distances. The paper investigates the case where they are probabilistic functions of the distances, in the asymptotic limit of a large number of voters. The key finding is that this inverts the evaluation of some voting rules, in particular Copeland and Random Dictatorship, for highly noisy voting.

Given the growing importance of social choice in machine learning, as well as accounting for noisy inputs while considering embedded vector spaces, I believe that this work scores high in significance.

Additionally, the analysis is quite thorough, with matching lower/upper asymptotic bounds for Plurality upper bound for Copeland and lower/upper bounds for Random Dictatorship.

The paper is also fairly well written.

Weaknesses:
My main concern is Lemma 3. The proof seems to argue that the constraints $\forall i, | b_i - w_i | \leq b_i + w_i$ (i.e. inequality constraint on $(i, W, B)$), but the optimization problem (7) has a constraint $\max_i | w_i - b_i | \leq \min_i b_i + w_i$. Thus if $\mathcal E_{\alpha}'$ was defined with all voter-wise constraints, then it would be a minimum over a smaller set, and thus $opt(\mathcal E_\alpha') \geq opt(\mathcal E_\alpha)$. Since Lemma 3's proof actually says $\frac{SC(W, d)}{SC(B, d)} \leq 1 / opt(\mathcal E'_\alpha)$, using this inequality seems to imply the actual Lemma 3. Am I reading this correctly?

It is disappointing that the upper-bound for Copeland. It would be helpful if the authors can point out where the argument gets loose.

Questions:
Could the authors clarify the proof of Lemma 3? I will happily increase my score given a convincing response.

Can the authors provide insight into why the Copeland upper bound is not tight?

More anecdotically, I wonder why "Metric Distortion" is restricted to the distortion of ranking-based algorithms. I feel that the concept is more general than that. Essentially, it is the worst social cost ratio of any voting rule, when executed on votes derived from distances in the metric space, right? In particular, it could be studied even for the social-cost minimizing geometric median, but given, e.g., noisy inputs or strategic votes? I believe that this is an interesting research venue, given that machine learning provides many tools to embed candidate features and voters' preferences in a vector space.

Rating:
7

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of metric distortion in single-winner elections. The key assumption is that the voters' preferences are not exactly compatible with the metric space, but they rather agree with it with a certain probability. The authors propose several axioms that formalize the requirements for the probability distribution for it to make sense in the context of distortion. Then they provide upper bounds of distortion in the probabilitic setting for Plurality, Random Dictator and Copeland (in case of the first two rules, they provide also lower bounds).
reviews.strengths: This work is the first one to combine probabilistic voting with metric distortion, hence the novelty is clear. The paper is overall of good quality, the axioms proposed in their work make sense to me, and the results are sound. The research direction introduced in this paper can be continued in further follow-up papers.
reviews.weaknesses:
The paper could have been more clearly written --- for example, the formal notation should be introduced at the beginning of Section 2   (together the model) rather than in the middle of the introduction.

Besides, I think that Axiom 2 (Independence of Other Candidates) could have been better motivated. I can imagine that it was crucial to obtain the authors' results, but it seems rather natural to me that  in the real-life scenarios that motivated the research, the presence of additional candidates can impact the voter's probability of ranking one candidate over another.

Another weakness is that the authors only consider three rules, and the analysis of only two of them is complete --- many important rules (like Borda or PluralityVeto) are not considered at all. This could raise a question whether this amount of technical contribution is enough for a top conference like NeurIPS.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and there are no potential negative societal impact of their work.

reviews.summary:
This paper considers metric distortion in probabilistic models of voting. In the metric distortion framework the voters and alternatives are embedded in a metric space, and given the ranked preferences the goal is to find an alternative with low distortion. In this setting these rankings come from a probabilistic model.

In the first part of the paper, there axioms are introduced and authors show which axiom is satisfied by which probabilisitic model. In the second half of the paper the goal is to find the distortion of Plurality and Copeland rules for a specific class of probabilistic models. The results show matching upper and lower bound of $m$ for plurality and constant upper bound for Copeland.
reviews.strengths:
Defining a model for distortion in stochastic models is a useful idea in future analysis of voting systems.
The idea of the paper is novel and it uses novel techniques in the second half.
I like the definition of the three axioms. I find them natural and easy to understand.
reviews.weaknesses:
My main concern is about the presentation. The preliminaries section is incomplete. The definition of distortion is hard to understand for a general audience and you made it harder by just putting the formula there. You have to add a description in words and give some intuition on why this definition makes sense. 

It's not clear how the probabilistic model works and how you define distortion on it until section 3 where you define it for a specific class. You have to formally define your probabilistic approach in Section 1.1 and also define distortion in this model. Not knowing the exact definition makes following the first paragraph of section 2 really hard. Before reading the rest of the paper I didn't understand why $P$ is a function of $d$ or why the preferences may not be consistent with the distances.

I think you have to add more intuition on the probabilisitic models. For instance you mention ground-truth in the definition of Mallows model but you have to explain in more details that how this model distributes the probabilities based on the distance to this ground-truth. The same explanations are needed for PQV.

My understanding is that the analysis that you provide works for any member of $G$, but currently the only members for which we have the final bound are PL and PQV. Is that true? If so is there any other interesting member of this class?
reviews.questions: See above.

reviews.summary:
This paper extends the framework of metric distortion, measuring how well voting rules minimize the social cost in a given metric space, to probabilistic voting scenarios where the preferences of voters are drawn from a probability distribution defined by the relative distances between candidates and each voter's ground truth position in the metric space.

The authors base their analysis on three different axioms that the induced marginal probabilities of relative preferences must verify, namely *scale-freeness*, *independence of other candidates* and *strict monotonicity.* They define a general class of marginal probabilities that verify the three axioms, and show that it encompasses the widely used *Plackett-Luce* model.

They then provide upper and lower bounds for the distortion of the *Plurality* rule, both linear in the number of candidates and matching asymptotically when the number of voters grows to infinity.

They then provide a upper-bound for the distortion of *Copeland* rule and show that it is independent of the number of candidates in the limit of a large number of voters.

Moreover, they give upper and (non-matching) lower-bounds for the distortion of *Random Dictator.*

They finally compare their results under both the *Plackett-Luce* and *Pairwise Quantal Voting* models (the latter being inspired form Quantal Response Theory), and show that the classical bounds of the metric distortion literature are recovered in the limit of vanishing randomness (although not for Copeland rule, hinting at a loose analysis).
reviews.strengths:
This paper, in extending the metric distortion analysis to probabilistic voting models perhaps closer to reality, is rather original and proposes a more optimistic view of metric distortion where randomized dictator is beatable in a worst case distortion sense.
The paper is fairly well written and the proofs seem correct.

It is an interesting idea, with an interesting result.
reviews.weaknesses:
The main problem of this paper is that it is not a good match to NeurIPS. This paper would work very well at an algorithms conference like SODA or ICALP, or a CS econ conference like EC (okay, probably one tier down like WINE), or possibly even at the those AI conferences that have a history in social choice theory like AAAI or IJCAI. And I also understand that NeurIPS has accepted such papers in the past. However, is it really a good fit for NeurIPS 2024? 

- There is no mention of the proof of Lemma 1 being in Appendix A.
- In the proof of Theorem 2, $\zeta$ is hardly introduced (also not in the Appendix).
- Formatting may be improved in place: e.g. Equations 6, 7, 10, or Theorem 3.
- l.312 "converges to 9 instead of 5". This part is not very clear, reminding the general bound in the deterministic case would improve readability.
- Typos:
	- l.220 "and is by solving".
	- Equation 24 showcases $(d)$ instead of $(a)$.
	- Equation 34: $\geq$ should be $=$.
	- l.618 "LEt".
	- l.641 should probably be deleted (equivalent to l.642).
	- l.660 weird grammar.
	- Footnote 6: missing index $\gamma_j$.
	- l.670: missing $(\hat{g}_{MID} +\hat{g}_{OUT}  )^2$ in inequalities $(a)$ and $(b).$ Furthermore, Equation 63 is used in $(a)$ rather than in $(b)$.
reviews.limitations: - The existence of distributions on rankings that generate pairwise order marginals of the form described in the paper is assumed and left for future work.

Strengths:
This paper, in extending the metric distortion analysis to probabilistic voting models perhaps closer to reality, is rather original and proposes a more optimistic view of metric distortion where randomized dictator is beatable in a worst case distortion sense.
The paper is fairly well written and the proofs seem correct.

And I also understand that NeurIPS has accepted such papers in the past. However, is it really a good fit for NeurIPS 2024? 

- There is no mention of the proof of Lemma 1 being in Appendix A.
- In the proof of Theorem 2, $\zeta$ is hardly introduced (also not in the Appendix).
- Formatting may be improved in place: e.g. Equations 6, 7, 10, or Theorem 3.
- l.312 "converges to 9 instead of 5". This part is not very clear, reminding the general bound in the deterministic case would improve readability.

title: Linear Mode Connectivity in Differentiable Tree Ensembles

reviews.summary: This paper aims to achieve LMC for soft tree ensembles. Akin to achieve LMC for neural network after accounting for permutation invariance, the authors introduce three different kinds of invariance in soft tree ensembles: tree permutation invariance, subtree flip invariance, splitting order invariance. Additionally, the authors demonstrate that better LMC can be achieved after considering all three kinds of invariance.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of extending LMC from neural networks to differentiable tree ensembles is interesting. 
2. Invariances beyond permutation variance are identified for differentiable tree ensembles. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of accounting for these invariances when doing matching.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. I am not familiar with differentiable tree ensembles, therefore, I would suggest the authors put more efforts on explaining tree ensembles and illustrating the invariances.
2. Another concern is about the motivation. This study is motivated by the question "Can LMC be achieved for soft tree ensembles?" but why would we achieve LMC for the tree ensembles? I would expect more elaboration on the motivation.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper aims to achieve LMC for soft tree ensembles. Akin to achieve LMC for neural network after accounting for permutation invariance, the authors introduce three different kinds of invariance in soft tree ensembles: tree permutation invariance, subtree flip invariance, splitting order invariance. Additionally, the authors demonstrate that better LMC can be achieved after considering all three kinds of invariance.

Strengths:
1. The idea of extending LMC from neural networks to differentiable tree ensembles is interesting. 
2. Invariances beyond permutation variance are identified for differentiable tree ensembles. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of accounting for these invariances when doing matching.

Weaknesses:
1. I am not familiar with differentiable tree ensembles, therefore, I would suggest the authors put more efforts on explaining tree ensembles and illustrating the invariances.
2. Another concern is about the motivation. This study is motivated by the question "Can LMC be achieved for soft tree ensembles?" but why would we achieve LMC for the tree ensembles? I would expect more elaboration on the motivation.

Rating:
6
reviews.text:
Review for “Linear Mode Connectivity in Differentiable Tree Ensembles”

Summary:  
This paper investigates whether Linear Mode Connectivity (LMC)—the phenomenon that linearly interpolating between two independently trained models yields consistently high performance—can be achieved for differentiable (soft) tree ensembles. Building on prior work in neural networks that accounts for permutation invariance of hidden neurons, the authors identify three key invariances intrinsic to tree architectures: (i) tree permutation invariance, (ii) subtree flip invariance (swapping left/right subtrees while inverting split thresholds via sign flips), and (iii) splitting order invariance (reordering shared splits in oblivious trees). They propose matching strategies based on Activation Matching (AM) and Weight Matching (WM) that exhaustively search over these invariances via the Linear Assignment Problem (LAP) with node‐wise weighting. Empirically, they demonstrate that accounting for all three invariances reduces the barrier (performance drop under interpolation) almost to zero on 16 tabular datasets (Tabular-Benchmark), for both oblivious and non-oblivious soft trees. Furthermore, they design a custom decision-list tree architecture without subtree flip or splitting order invariance, showing that with just permutation invariance one can still achieve LMC—enabling more efficient model merging. The paper is— to our knowledge—the first to establish LMC in soft tree ensembles and to highlight the importance of architecture-specific invariances beyond permutation.

Strengths:  
1. Extension of LMC Beyond Neural Nets: The core idea of transferring the LMC concept from neural networks to soft tree ensembles is novel and timely. Given the growing interest in integrating tree models with deep learning, this work fills a clear gap.  
2. Identification of Tree-Specific Invariances: Subtree flip and splitting order invariances are clearly defined (Section 3.1, Figure 2) and distinguished from standard permutation invariance. This taxonomy deepens our understanding of equivariances in non-neural architectures.  
3. Matching Methodology: The adaptation of AM and WM to trees using the LAP, combined with a thoughtful node-weighting scheme based on leaf reachability (Figure 3), is methodically sound. Algorithms 1–2 in Appendix A provide sufficient detail for reproduction.  
4. Comprehensive Empirical Study: Experiments span 16 real-world tabular datasets (Table 5), multiple tree depths (D=1–3), tree counts (M=64–1024), and both train/test splits (Figures 5–7, 9–24). Reporting of mean ± std across five seed pairs, along with barriers and interpolation curves, gives a clear picture of robustness.  
5. Practical Architecture Design: The decision-list variant (Figure 4, Table 1) demonstrates that one can sacrifice certain invariances for computational efficiency, a useful insight for scalable model merging in resource-constrained settings.  

Weaknesses & Suggestions for Improvement:  
1. Background on Differentiable Trees and Invariances (Original Point):  
   – The paper assumes familiarity with soft tree ensembles, but readers less versed in tree models may struggle with notation and the role of shared parameters in oblivious trees (Section 2.2, Equations 2–4). For instance, the formulation of μₘ,ℓ(xᵢ, wₘ, bₘ) in Equation (2) and the distinction between hard vs. soft splits could benefit from a concrete toy example.  
   – It would strengthen the presentation to add a paragraph early on contrasting standard decision trees (hard routing) with soft routing, perhaps illustrating how a single tree’s output is computed via leaf weights πₘℓ and soft assignment μₘ,ℓ. A small schematic or step-by-step example for D=2 could clarify how subtree flip and splitting order operations leave the function unchanged.  
   – The invariances are introduced in Figure 2 and Section 3.1, but an extended discussion of when each invariance arises (e.g., non-oblivious vs. oblivious settings) and how they interact in practice would make the concepts more accessible.  

2. Motivation for LMC in Tree Ensembles (Original Point):  
   – While the question “Can LMC be achieved for soft tree ensembles?” motivates the study, the broader significance of achieving LMC in non-neural models deserves more emphasis. In the introduction (currently pp.1–2), the authors mention model merging (e.g., model soups [6], task arithmetic [7]) and stability of non-convex optimization. However, a paragraph explicitly outlining practical scenarios where tree model merging is crucial—such as federated learning with tree models, fast ensemble updates in online systems, or unlearning in decision-tree-based recommender systems—would clarify why LMC matters beyond theoretical interest.  
   – The paper could also relate to the literature on hierarchical mixtures of experts (Section 1, l.23–31) and large-scale language models (l.32–37), by explaining how tree-based components might be merged or averaged efficiently if LMC holds. This would underscore the cross-architecture relevance of the findings.  

3. Scalability of Matching Algorithms:  
   – Section 3.2 acknowledges that brute-force enumeration of 2^(2D−1) sign flips and D! splitting order permutations becomes intractable for large D. While the decision-list architecture addresses this for shallow models, readers may wonder about heuristic or approximate matching for deeper soft trees in high-dimensional settings. Adding a short discussion or pointing toward potential sampling-based or greedy heuristics for large trees would round out the practical considerations.  

4. Additional Ablations and Interpretability:  
   – The paper demonstrates that barriers decrease as tree count M increases (Figure 5, right column) and increase with depth D. It would be informative to see whether certain invariances dominate the reduction in barrier—for example, is subtree flip or splitting order more critical for oblivious vs. non-oblivious trees? A bar chart quantifying the marginal benefit of each invariance (e.g., Δ barrier when adding flip vs. order invariance) could add nuance.  
   – Given that tree ensembles often serve interpretability goals, a brief commentary on whether matched trees remain interpretable after applying these invariances (e.g., swapping subtrees) could be useful.  

Overall, the paper offers a compelling and thorough exploration of LMC in tree-based models. The identified invariances, matching strategies, and novel decision-list variant collectively advance both theoretical understanding and practical model-merging techniques. With expanded background exposition on soft tree models and a more detailed motivation for why LMC matters in tree ensembles, the manuscript will be even more accessible and impactful.  

Rating: 6  
(Consistent with the original review decision.)
